data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Dips
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.8 percentage points to 37.7%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in 16 weeks and is above 30% for only the seventh time this year. Bullish sentiment was last higher on January 30, 2025 (41.0%).

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.9 percentage points to 25.6%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 44th time in 46 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.7 percentage points to 36.7%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 25th time in 27 weeks. Bearish sentiment was last lower on January 30, 2025 (34.0%).

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 9.5 percentage points to 1.0%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the first time in 16 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they would describe the updated earnings guidance given by companies during the first-quarter 2025 earnings season.

Here is how they responded:

  • Better than I expected: 28.2%.
  • Approximately what I expected: 41.7%.
  • Worse than I expected: 11.5%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 18.3%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 37.7%, up 1.8 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 25.6%, up 5.9 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 36.7%, down 7.7 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Still A Solid Dividend Stock
data.description:
When I last covered Trinity Capital (NASDAQ:TRIN), I noted that early signs of credit stabilization began to appear. Since then, TRIN’s risk rating on its portfolio has remained largely stable. While this may not represent a strong sustained improvement yet, it is noteworthy in a time period where many of its peers are facing increased portfolio risks as higher-for-longer interest rates have made it more difficult for many borrowers to service their debt.

The portfolio and asset quality

When I last covered TRIN, its weighted average internal risk rating across its portfolio stood at 2.9, where it remains as of its most recently announced results. TRIN uses a detailed framework to assess the likelihood of loan defaults, based on seven core factors. Management assigns a risk rating to each investment in the portfolio on a scale from 1 to 5. During the evaluation, management takes into account liquidity or cash flow, how the actual performance meets performance expectations, the value of collateral supporting the investment, uniqueness of the product, among other factors. Investments rated between 4.0 and 5.0 are classified as high performers, while those rated between 1.0 and 1.5 are considered to be in default or going through a restructuring phase. These risk scores provide investors with a glimpse into management’s assessment of the likelihood of default across the portfolio.

A stable internal risk rating suggests that the overall portfolio has continued to perform in line with management's recent expectations. Perhaps more importantly the percentage of the portfolio that has performed above management’s expectations has increased since the time of my previous coverage, with the percentage of the portfolio assigned a rating of 3.0 – 3.9 having increased from just under 30% to 34% in the first quarter of 2025. This suggests that while the overall risk weighting of the portfolio has remained the same, there is an improved outlook in certain pockets of the portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio on the watchlist has also declined slightly from 3.7% at the time of my previous coverage to 3% in the first quarter of 2025. In my view, these factors considered together point to further improvement in portfolio quality along with an overall lower risk of default.

However, in recent months there has been growing concern over an overall challenging operating environment for the BDC sector. Fitch recently observed that 2025 was likely to witness a ‘deteriorating’ sector outlook for BDCs, citing expectations of increased competition in underwriting, declining net investment income (NII) and dividend coverage, as well as the potential for rising non-accruals and credit losses due to elevated interest rates and a tough economic environment. The risk of changing tariff policies has also added to the uncertainty. While these are all material risks that should be monitored closely, they do not presently present a major concern in respect of TRIN’s portfolio.

Concerning tariffs, management recently noted that:

We've been closely monitoring the recent tariff announcements and have been in discussions with all of our portfolio companies to determine the potential impact on their operational performance. Credit quality is of the utmost importance to us, particularly during periods of market volatility. The portfolio management team is actively engaged with every single one of our portfolio companies to analyze the effects of tariffs, quantify the potential impact across our all risk factors and safeguard the health of our investments… An overwhelming majority of our portfolio companies are domestically headquartered and have very limited exposure to imported goods or international sales. As such, most do not expect a near term impact to operations as a direct result of tariffs imposed by the United States or other countries.

While this doesn’t mean that tariffs cannot have other knock-on effects on its portfolio companies, the direct impact will at least be limited.

The dividend and earnings

TRIN offers a dividend yield of more than 14%, which is the highest of the BDCs included in the peer-comp chart below. Its dividend also remains fully covered by NII, with its most recent coverage at 102%. Nevertheless, this coverage ratio is lower than at the time of my previous coverage, at which time the coverage ratio was around 106%.

This lower coverage ratio is not ideal but could see improvement as the effect of lowered interest rates seem to have been worked in. Management noted in this respect that "we're actually in a really great position going forward as it relates to rate cuts, having the majority of our portfolio really already at kind of base floor rates". This coupled with the fact that TRIN's portfolio has been growing again, with new originations exceeding repayments, offers the potential for earnings growth that makes the dividend more sustainable. Importantly, the bulk of TRIN's NII is also made up of actual cash-income, with less than 2.5% of its total investment income made up of PIK income.

Valuation and Conclusion

TRIN is currently trading at a premium to NAV of around 13%. As at the time of my previous article, this is higher than its 3-year average premium to NAV of around 5%. However, at the time I noted that I don’t consider the stock to be overvalued as its recent track record of solid NII, prudent risk management, and sustained dividends warrant the revaluation to its current premium to NAV. If the BDC can sustain NII growth at a healthy pace while maintaining asset quality, an additional re-rating could also be justified. Based on the recent results, I continue to hold this position.

Trinity Capital continues to demonstrate relative portfolio stability in a challenging BDC environment marked by rising credit concerns and macroeconomic headwinds. The internal risk rating of TRIN’s portfolio has remained unchanged at 2.9, but there are encouraging signs of improvement in asset quality, with a greater portion of investments now performing above management’s expectations and a slight reduction in watchlist exposure. While broader sector concerns such as rising non-accrual risks, tariff policy changes, and declining NII remain, TRIN appears to be navigating these pressures effectively, with management remaining proactive and transparent about emerging risks.

With a dividend yield exceeding 14%, full NII coverage, and relatively low reliance on PIK income, TRIN remains well-positioned to deliver consistent income to shareholders. Although dividend coverage has modestly declined since my previous coverage, portfolio growth and the diminishing drag from base-rate resets could support improving NII going forward. The stock's current 13% premium to NAV remains justifiable in light of its strong operational performance and prudent risk posture. I therefore reiterate my Buy rating on TRIN, with continued confidence in its ability to generate shareholder value through disciplined underwriting and resilient earnings.

data.title: Enphase: Trump Tax Bill Crashes The Solar Sector - What's Next
data.description:
Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH) plunged after the House of Representatives approved the tax and spending bill, adding to pressures from tariffs and a downturn. The company also appears to be facing competitive pressures that may be calling the historical competitive dynamics into question. I am tempering back my bullishness in light of the projected margin impact from tariffs, but remain optimistic that the stock can work out over the long term. I rate the stock a buy, but volatility may be ahead.

ENPH Stock Price

I last covered ENPH in March, when I rated the stock a Strong Buy as I envisioned a cyclical recovery. Tariffs and politics have spoiled that party, with the stock falling 37% since then.

Cracks are forming in the bullish thesis, especially in the near to medium term, but the cheap valuation is making it hard to get away from this one.

ENPH Stock Key Metrics

ENPH is an integral player in the solar sector, selling microinverters used to efficiently make solar power usable for everyday applications. Historically, the importance of these products in the solar lifecycle has afforded ENPH consistently elevated growth rates and margins, especially due to the supposed reliability advantages over the traditional string inverter model. The last year, however, has called this notion into question largely due to competition from Tesla (TSLA), which ironically employs strong inverters.

The company’s diversified global supply chain (aka not every factory is in China) has helped somewhat insulate the company from the tariff war, but that is only true for their microinverters. The company’s battery products (needed given TSLA’s push for an all-in-one solution) are still largely manufactured in China and this is expected to have an impact on margins for the foreseeable future.

In the most recent quarter, the company delivered $356.1 million in revenue, coming in at the low end of guidance of between $340 million to $380 million in revenue. The last several quarters has seen management struggle to guide this business as they have not delivered the “beat and raise” quarters that investors grew accustomed to over the last decade. The main problem is that this downturn has proved more elongated and vicious than prior cycles. The company did see margins recover solidly over the prior year, with non-GAAP operating margins nearly doubling.

The company was also profitable on a GAAP basis, an impressive feat given that the solar industry has been in a rut for a while now.

The company’s ability to sustain solid margins over the past several quarters has been an important differentiating factor versus close peer SolarEdge (SEDG) and formed a large part of my bullish thesis. The problem is that the strong margins are going to come under pressure due to tariffs. On the conference call, management noted that the impact from tariffs is expected to reduce gross margins by 200 bps in the second quarter and by 6% to 8% in the third quarter. The second quarter is expected to see less impact due to the company having more inventory on hand. Management hopes to “fully offset the impact starting in Q2 '26” but investors can be forgiven for retaining a dose of skepticism, given that this management team has seemingly been calling for a cyclical recovery every quarter for over a year now.

The company ended the quarter with $1.53 billion of cash versus $1.2 billion of debt. I expect this net cash position to come under pressure as margins face tariff headwinds over the near future. The company repurchased $100 million of stock at an average price of $62.71 per share.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the second quarter to see revenue of between $340 million to $380 million. That represents some growth over the $303.5 million posted last year, but represents a moderated pace of recovery. For reference, the company generated $711.1 million of revenue in the June quarter in 2023.

What’s more, roughly $40 million of that revenue is expected to be “Safe Harbor” revenue, which essentially refers to pre-booking of business beyond the current year. These all occurred prior to the House passing the tax bill but back then it was already clear that the recovery is facing serious roadblocks. The further problem is that the tax bill threatens to remove the 30% federal tax credit for clean energy installations, a move that would undoubtedly crush demand for new solar installations. I find it likely that ENPH issues poor guidance for the third quarter, even if it might benefit from some pull-forward in demand as there would be a 60-day leeway period if the bill is enacted.

Is ENPH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, ENPH traded at under 16x earnings.

I have previously characterized this as a cheap valuation due to it being based on down-cycle earnings. However, consensus estimates are no longer projecting a rapid recovery, as the company is not expected to return to peak levels until many years later.

What’s more, tariffs and the potential remove of the federal tax credits have joined competitive pressures in adding to headwinds to profit margins. I am now lowering my long term margin assumption to 20%. For reference, consensus estimates call for around 21% margins this year - and that would be at a supposed cyclical low. I do not see a quick recovery as tariffs have increased the risk of inflation, reducing the likelihood of a reduction in interest rates. Furthermore, if the tax bill were to pass the senate (and thus removing the federal tax credits), ENPH might see growth turn substantially negative over the coming year. The company might see around 5% to 10% annual growth thereafter - I would not be surprised if growth falls short of consensus given competitive pressures.

In the current interest rate environment, I see 15x earnings (3x sales) as being fair value. That said, the business would likely recover rapidly in the event of falling interest rates, leading me to construct a fair value range of 3x to 6x sales. If the removal of the federal tax credits were to lead to a 25% decline in sales, then the stock would trade at around 4.6x sales, right at the middle of that range. ENPH remains buyable, but I must temper my bullishness due to the lack of positive catalysts in the near term.

ENPH Stock Risks

It is possible that ENPH will be unable to overcome competition from TSLA. It is not clear how TSLA’s string inverters have managed to overcome the reliability gap previously seen from other string inverter competitors. TSLA also has more financial resources that may enable it to compete on price from an advantageous position. ENPH is likely to see ongoing volatility as its earnings power can continue to deteriorate under worsening macro conditions. It is possible that the business suffers a greater hit from the federal tax credits than projected.

ENPH Stock Conclusion

ENPH has done an exemplary job in protecting its profit margins but tariffs and the potential for the removal of federal tax credits may make it difficult to continue this strong performance. The company has a slim net cash balance sheet and investors should pay close attention to signs of financial stress. The stock trades very cheaply, helping to compensate for some of these risks. The low valuation and solid financial metrics are key factors that I require for holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I rate the stock a buy but the near term picture remains quite.

data.title: 5 Things To Consider After Lumen's Mass Markets Fiber Asset Sale To AT&T
data.description:
Since peaking at $10.33 last November 2024, Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN) shares perked up after a deal with AT&T (T). After stock markets closed on May 21, 2025, AT&T announced that it would buy Lumen’s mass markets fiber business for $5.75 billion.

Why are Lumen shares up sharply after this news? What should investors do next? Investors have five things to consider.

1/ Market Reaction to Deal is Positive

The deal is mutually beneficial, with investors liking the deal. AT&T shares gained fractionally by 0.27% after the company said the deal is “immaterial” to its adjusted EBITDA, earnings per share (“EPS”), and free cash flow. However, Lumen’s fiber business is accretive in the long term.

In contrast, LUMN stock rose by 8.53% at the time of writing. Chief Finance Officer Chris Stansbury said that although it is a great asset, this asset is a better fit for a firm that has a wireless offering. More importantly, Lumen shareholders are receptive to the deal for a better reason: debt reduction.

2/ Lumen Reduced Debt

In the first quarter, Lumen’s affiliate Level 3 Financing refinanced its $2.4 billion term loan debt. Although it ended the quarter with $17.334 billion in long-term debt, it had a strong liquidity profile. It held cash and cash equivalents of $1.9 billion.

The asset sale will improve Lumen’s balance sheet. Last week, before the company announced the deal, CFO Stansbury acknowledged the debt problem. He said, “We are the poster child for that, given what we went through a couple of years ago around the debt because you can't outrun that problem.” In comparison, Lumen’s competitors did not leverage its balance sheet as much.

To outflank the competition, Lumen reduced the number of hops that customers needed to pay to connect to the cloud. To avoid relying upon a network that became a commodity, Lumen, like its competition, sold off those assets. Lumen shareholders should expect the company to pivot enabling the build-out of digital service on top of the network.

Opportunities

Enterprises require more than 100G bandwidth, pushing toward 400 G. Cisco Systems (CSCO), Ciena (CIEN), Juniper Networks (JNPR), and Arista Networks (ANET) are some of the firms that supply 400G network infrastructure. In addition, Coherent (COHR) specializes in photonics and optical networking solutions. I covered COHR stock in the past (you may click on my profile and search for them). In the table below, COHR stock has strong scores in all categories:

On March 31, Lumen and Ciena said they collaborated to successfully transport 1.2 Tbps (terabit wavelength service) across 3,050 kilometers (1,800 miles). This is the longest 1.2 Tbps of non-generated signal across the globe. Lumen said that this trial is important, since artificial intelligence (AI), cloud, and security require data transmission speeds toward new limits.

In effect, Lumen’s 1.2 Tbps wavelength service demand will meet next-gen AI requirements. It will also support applications that are data intensive. Customers will have scalability and greater network efficiency. As a result, it will increase Lumen’s revenue per customer.

3/ $15 Billion Total Addressable Market

CEO Kate Johnson said at the Annual JPMorgan Global Technology, Media and Communications Conference that the lower-speed services, in partnership with Google (GOOG), is a $15 billion TAM. Customers benefit from the services as do cloud providers. The latter may ramp up the capacity by enabling on-ramps. Lumen SASE with Fortinet (FTNT) is an example of an onramp.

Looking ahead, Lumen’s infrastructure is in a good position to enable the company to build strong relationships with hyperscalers. Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and Microsoft (MSFT) are examples of firms that are dominant players in the hyperscale market. Customers rely on their infrastructure to support their AI endeavors.

Here are the stock grades on the aforementioned firms in the hyperscale and cloud space:

4/ Risks

The proverbial “sell the news” is a near-term risk for Lumen’s stock price. Since peaking at over $10 last November, LUMN stock trended lower. At the open, the stock traded at $4.40 but by around noon, the stock rose by around 1.5%.

Traders will book the quick profits, while long-term investors need not take action. AT&T benefits from asset purchases. By comparison, when BCE (BCE) bought Zipfly for $3.65 billion for a similar number of customers, its shares fell by around 10%. It traded at $40.47 back then, compared to around $21.50 today.

5/ Stock Rating

Lumen scores a “Buy” on Seeking Alpha's quant rating.

The stock has a strong valuation and growth rating. Analysts raised their EPS, giving the stock a “B” on the EPS revision.

In the last three months, eight analysts raised their revenue forecast for Lumen.

Your Takeaway

Despite the volatile trading in Lumen Technologies today, the company’s asset sale is a welcome development for shareholders. Lumen continues to execute on growing sales, expanding profit margins, and cutting costs. It may use the funds to lower its debt and invest in its next-generation infrastructure. The demand for AI is not slowing down. Customers will want high-performance, resilient networks in the AI economy. Lumen is the firm that will provide the backbone to that.

data.title: Water Risks - An Investor's Guide To Navigating Sustainable Water Management
data.description:
Executive Summary

Water security is a growing risk for companies, forcing investors to recognize and address their exposures. Failure to manage water risks can inflict material damage on business and investment outcomes, while companies that are strategically focused on the issue could enjoy tangible competitive advantages. Investors seeking to manage material water risks have so far relied largely on disclosure requirements to influence corporate behavior. But a data-disclosure approach—though valuable—has notable limitations. To overcome this problem, we propose stakeholder management, an approach that focuses on engaging local stakeholders to address water risks. By combining the disclosure regime with better awareness of local needs, we believe companies can make their water stewardship more effective. Our analysis of companies in three water risk–prone industries—metals & mining, beverages and semiconductor manufacturing—led us to develop a versatile, five-part framework for investors. This framework is designed to both assess and improve companies’ stakeholder management practices. We believe our approach provides an opportunity for investors to engage1 with companies and help guide them through the complexities of water security toward more sustainable and value-enhancing outcomes.

A Real-World Liquidity Crisis Water security represents a critical challenge for the world: how to address too much, too little and too-polluted water. These triple hazards threaten damage and disruption across multiple areas, such as economic development, public health, food security and social stability. A rising world population, climate changes that outstrip adaptation measures and competition among water users will likely intensify the problems. United Nations (UN) experts forecast a 40% global shortfall in freshwater supply by 2030, if current consumption and production don’t evolve.2 Data from the World Resources Institute’s Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas reveal that 25 countries, home to one-quarter of the world's population, experience severe water stress each year and repeatedly deplete nearly all available water supplies, endangering regional water access and resilience (Display 1).3

These risks to our planet can have a material impact on companies and their investors. According to CDP Worldwide (CDP), water risks could substantially impact the business of 69% of listed equities, potentially compromising up to US$225 billion of value.4 The most reported potential threats to business operations include: reduced or disrupted production capacity; increased operating costs; lower sales/output; supply chain disruption; closure of operations; and constraints to growth (Display 2). Water risks can also affect the financial institutions that back companies. Taken together, we believe these hazards represent a potentially material threat to the cash flows and earnings of an array of companies around the world, warranting careful consideration from investors seeking to forecast long-term return potential. By engaging in constructive dialogue with corporate management teams, we believe active investors can help mitigate water risks to companies and thus to portfolios.

Finding a Way Forward While the current trajectory of water security points toward a bleak future, it’s not too late to change course to manage and minimize risk exposures. In this paper, we aim to highlight how local social considerations for water management can influence corporate resilience to water risks and introduce an investor framework for mitigating such risks. We present research on different management approaches to water security, real-world examples of water-related operational disruptions and proprietary engagement case studies. Our paper:

• showcases the role that engagement with local stakeholders plays in identifying and managing water risks,

• reviews the degree of preparedness in three sectors with high exposure to the water crisis (metals & mining, beverages, and semiconductor manufacturing) (Display 3) and

• uses this evidence to formulate an adaptive investor framework that leverages local stakeholder considerations for corporate engagement on water risks.

Using data disclosure alone as a tool to drive corporate behavior has limitations,5 but stakeholder management offers a way to redress those shortfalls, in our view. By pairing these two approaches, we believe companies can more effectively integrate water objectives into their operations with a view toward reducing risks (Display 4).

Why Reliance on Data Is Insufficient

Water security involves complex issues. Though data disclosure is important, stakeholder management can identify risks that disclosure-based approaches may miss.

Data disclosure creates transparency, which in turn can drive corporate behavior. Transparency also creates the potential for accountability, which is a crucial component of good governance.

Disclosure requirements and standards that promote transparency around corporate water use are rising globally. These include: the EU taxonomy, Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures, CDP, Global Reporting Initiative, International Financial Reporting Standards and Science Based Targets Network. Nonetheless, as described by the UN’s CEO Water Mandate, data can be, and often are, quite lacking in many regards.6 Data disclosure–based approaches face four key pitfalls:

1. Water is a complex, multidimensional resource. There’s no single equivalent target for water comparable to the Paris Agreement’s 1.5ºC threshold relating to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions for climate. Water use can have a wide range of impacts on water availability and quality that vary depending on the location and type of water source, the water source’s specific characteristics, and the services that water source provides. Given the intricacy and variability of water-related impacts, no single target can capture these impacts in a meaningful, comprehensive way.

2. Water is a local issue. Whereas one ton of CO2 has the same impact regardless of where it’s emitted, water impacts are site-specific. The same interference with water resources can generate different outcomes depending on the location of that intervention. For example, discharging treated wastewater in parts of Alaska could exacerbate water stress because the melting permafrost and increased precipitation could cause stronger river flows and higher levels of dissolved solids, which are correlated to the incidence of certain health conditions. Conversely, releasing treated wastewater in depleted river basins in Arizona could help alleviate water stress.

3. The existing data on water management may be insufficient. Given that water management and accounting may not have been primary strategic concerns until recently, companies are still in the process of implementing proper data collection systems. As a result, both internal and supplier data may lack maturity. Also, data obtained from external databases to address gaps in proprietary data may not be specific enough. Such data could exhibit averages that provide limited visibility on whether or not a particular company was a better manager of water resources than average.7

4. Societal and political factors are often missed. Even when data is sufficient, quantitative analysis does not reveal other societal and political factors, such as how a project is received by affected communities.8 By examining the risk of water-related operational disruptions - including stranded assets - faced by mining, beverages and semiconductor manufacturing companies, corporate engagement with local stakeholders assumes a paramount role in navigating water risks (see page 8).

Three Industries at Risk

1. Mining First Quantum Minerals was forced to close its copper mine in Panama in 2023 after total investments of at least US$10 billion. The closure followed mass public protests, in part over environmental concerns that the mine threatened water supplies in a biodiverse area. Similarly, in 2013, the Chilean government reversed environmental approval of Barrick Gold’s Pascua-Lama gold and silver mine after its US$8 billion investment. The mine disrupted glacial meltwater that fed a river basin sustaining the irrigation needs of 70,000 small-scale farmers.9

2. Beverages Constellation Brands pursued construction of a third brewery in Mexicali, Mexico, despite protests from farmers that the third brewery would exacerbate an already low water table. The Mexican government denied Constellation the required water permits. Constellation lost US$670 million of the US$690 million it had invested.10

3. Semiconductor Manufacturing Semiconductor manufacturers are already encountering major water issues, and 40% of all current manufacturing facilities are located in watersheds expected to experience severe water stress by 2030.11 The manufacturing process necessitates ultrapure water (UPW) to clear impurities. About 2,200 gallons of UPW (around 3,520 gallons of unpurified water) are used in the production of a standard 300mm wafer. Semiconductor manufacturers may face increasingly significant water issues and need to work alongside local stakeholders to navigate issuance of this limited resource, as more facilities are built in water-stressed areas. For example, owing to US incentives, facilities are being built in the Colorado River Basin despite its desiccation.

Taking Local Stakeholders Seriously

Local stakeholder management—collaborating with affected communities and municipal governments—is vital to ensuring proper water management and maintaining a license to operate. Nature restoration projects have successfully applied this approach through stakeholder inclusion. For instance, in reforestation, the most cited causes of project problems or failures were conflicting goals between local communities and project managers and a lack of community involvement. Stakeholder engagement features effective engagement with a broad range of stakeholders regarding water management throughout the lifecycle of a project and beyond. We believe the time is right for companies to focus on stakeholder management in order to address the material risks posed by water security. The European Union (EU) law Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), adopted in January 2023, requires companies to assess the impact of their water use on affected communities’ water access and rights, and to disclose whether and how the perspectives of affected communities are factored into a company’s decision-making processes.12 These EU standards go further than other measures, as they are mandatory, require a higher level of detail, investigate both potential and actual impacts, and focus on double materiality, a concept that requires companies to take into account not just the financial ESG risks they may face but also their environmental and social impact.13 The effective date of CSRD reporting requirements for the first tranche of companies (including those not based in the EU) will now likely be 2028, in order to allow for changes requested by the European Commission. In addition to codifying the link between water management and affected communities, we believe these CSRD initiatives provide further impetus for combining local stakeholder input with disclosure governance.

Because the new CSRD disclosure requirements seek to gather tangible, qualitative detail on action plans, it will be critical for both companies and investors to assess these plans and their outcomes. According to the PwC 2024 Global CSRD Survey, companies state that data availability and quality are the biggest obstacles to implementation (Display 5). The breadth and depth of CSRD reporting requirements present a massive challenge as teams work to collect, verify and consolidate many new types of data.14 Companies have low confidence in their ability to satisfy reporting obligations for water and marine resources and for affected communities, with 11% and 14% respectively of in-scope companies questioning whether they could meet these reporting requirements satisfactorily.15 Corporate uncertainty makes investor engagement and stakeholder management especially timely.

Creating a Framework for Stakeholder Management

By collating insights gleaned from engagements with companies, we’ve created a five-part adaptive framework that investors can use to:

• assess companies’ stakeholder management practices and, by inference, their water risk management profiles and

• encourage companies to bolster their stakeholder management practices to mitigate their water risk exposures.

The Framework

Recognition: Acknowledgment of the social context of water usage is a prerequisite for stakeholder management.16 Key features include:

  • The company is committed to treating access to water as a human right, with clear supporting policies and codes of conduct.
  • The board and senior management demonstrate an understanding of, and focus on, mitigating water risks, fostering transparency, and integrating stakeholder engagement to optimize water use and enhance business competitiveness.

Accountability & Oversight: Clear assignment of responsibilities coupled with performance-monitoring systems facilitate program implementation. Key features include:

Mechanisms exist for evaluating, documenting and reporting on water and stakeholder management.

Accountability extends across the company’s own operations and value chain with clear consequences for mismanagement.

Evidence of board-level oversight of relevant policies, systems of implementation, performance and assurance.

Resourcing: Synthesis of expertise across a range of domains and seniority supports effective agenda setting and project realization.17 Key actions include: | Implementing top-down and bottom-up approaches to water and stakeholder management. Daily riskmanagement tasks are performed by business units and communicated to the leadership team, while the leadership team passes down macro-objectives to the business units.18 | The cultivating and integrating of water- and stakeholder-management expertise across the business. Ensure qualified individuals participate in water and stakeholder activities both at the enterprise and local levels.

Stakeholder Mapping: Identifying and assessing local stakeholders bolsters risk identification. Key actions include: | Conducting environmental and social risk assessments to identify whether the company is operating in areas of water stress and to evaluate potential ensuing social risks; and | Accounting for the wide range of local stakeholders. The assessment of communities as heterogenous units with various interests and actors is key to identifying the part each plays in decision-making.19 Stakeholder Engagement: Collaboration with local stakeholders enhances awareness of varying realities on-site that can advance action plans to mitigate risks.20 Key actions include: | Adopting a participatory, iterative process that extends throughout the project lifecycle and beyond; | Investing in locating expertise on the ground to facilitate stakeholder engagement;21 | Translating knowledge gleaned from engagements into appropriate, timely and measurable action plans and targets; and | Hosting a centralized system of records to enable adaptive management of stakeholder engagements and ongoing evaluation of engagements and their outcomes.22

Illustrative Case Studies

Our detailed discussions with companies enable us to gain insight into their approaches to engagement with local stakeholders, assess their practices and advocate for enhanced stakeholder management. The case studies presented below summarize the types of discussions we have hosted with companies across our portfolios. After processing raw data, we selected three companies— Teck Resources (Teck), Pernod Ricard (Pernod) and Analog Devices (ADI)—for our case studies due to their elevated water security risk exposure and potential for enhanced management oversight.

The industries in which these companies operate—manufacturing; materials; and food, beverage & agriculture—were among the top four reporting the highest number of operation closures due to water risks, according to CDP.23 Following detailed discussions with companies to gain insights into their stakeholder management practices, we created a scorecard that assesses how well their corporate practices align with the five criteria in our investor framework. This scorecard enables continuous evaluation, identifies areas for improvement, tracks progress and facilitates comparisons across different corporations.

Teck Resources: Mining with Mindfulness Toward the Community

Teck Resources (TECK), a Canadian mining company, relies on water for materials processing. If managed poorly, its water consumption can impact water quality and availability for other stakeholders in the watershed.

By acknowledging access to water as a human right, Teck has laid the foundation for mindful water stewardship.

Its approach is based on three dictums: use water efficiently; protect water quality; and collaborate with local communities to manage shared water resources. The company’s commitment to community engagement is formalized in its Code of Sustainable Conduct.

Setback Triggers Remedial Actions

Greater water awareness can sometimes be fostered by controversy. Around 12 years ago, Teck suffered selenium leakage from its Elk Valley mining operations in British Columbia (BC). This caused concerns among municipalities, scientists and indigenous communities (including the Ktunaxa) over poisoning of native fisheries. The company responded by enhancing its water stewardship and community engagement programs and implementing the Elk Valley Water Quality Plan, which was approved in 2014 by the BC Government. When put into action, the plan ultimately treated 77.5 million liters of water per day and was overseen by over 200 people.

Communication efforts with the local community were integral to the company’s water stewardship plans. This included direct communication between Teck’s senior vice president (SVP) of Sustainability and External Affairs and the Ktunaxa chief, as well as increased open hours and on-site expertise. The company also implemented formal grievance mechanisms and committed to obtaining consent before commencing new projects or making significant changes.

Over time, these actions led to more than 100 formal agreements between Teck and indigenous communities, up from five in the past decade. Despite spinning off its steelmaking coal business in Elk Valley in 2023, we believe that Teck has continued to pursue effective water stewardship and community engagement as it expands copper mining operations in water-scarce Chile, where eight of its nine mines are in or near indigenous territories.

Actions Backed by Appropriate Resources

At each site, Teck has a team with dedicated leads who oversee water stewardship and community relationships and report to the project manager. With input from site leads, Teck creates an area-of-interest map for each operation to identify affected communities. Teck’s risk registry scales risks from 0 to 5 based on likelihood and severity; the project board leverages this risk registry to develop action plans to minimize risk exposure. Compliance with enterprise standards at each site is tracked through a dashboard and assured through audits.

The project manager is ultimately accountable for effective water stewardship and community engagement, although each practitioner’s performance criteria feature two to three metrics relevant to that person’s domain. Beyond the site, regional leads for community, environment and regulatory and government affairs meet regularly with the SVP for each region. Meanwhile, the chief sustainability officer brings site-level experience to her global role, and the board of directors oversees policies, practices, performance and assurances through its Safety and Sustainability Committee.

Finding substitute water sources in water-scarce regions is another important policy goal. Teck set an enterprise- level target to use seawater or low-quality water sources for all operations in water-scarce regions by 2040. In line with that ambition, Teck built a seawater desalination facility at its Chile-based Quebrada Blanca Phase 2 copper project to avoid impacting local water sources and water users. The facility will service all production processes and potentially produce excess water to be shared with local communities.

Despite facing water-quality controversies, Teck has shown a keen understanding of the challenges underpinning them. The company has developed dedicated teams, systems and technologies to mitigate selenium leakage. Furthermore, Teck has integrated stakeholder management into its water stewardship efforts by recognizing access to water as a human right, mapping diverse stakeholder needs, setting standards and commitments, engaging affected communities, establishing monitoring and assurance processes, and implementing this integrated approach across varying levels of management. We believe that Teck has demonstrated a dedication to continuous learning and enhancement, as it continues to invest in and expand these initiatives and apply them to new mining projects.

Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF): Drinking Responsibly

As a leading global wines and spirits producer, Pernod Ricard (Pernod) relies on water as a vital raw material in both its beverage production and agricultural supply chain. The French company acknowledges the crucial role local communities play in watershed management and identifies potential stakeholder conflicts over local water resources as a risk to agribusinesses along its value chain.

Social responsibility is actively pursued from the top down. Pernod’s board of directors and executive committee regularly convene a corporate social responsibility meeting. The Sustainability and Responsibility Steering Committee, comprising senior representatives from multiple business functions, mandates resilience plans and relevant roadmaps for headquarter functions. To enhance the roadmaps, Headquarter Operations uses its expertise and collaborates with affiliates, each of which has a Sustainability Lead, on local implementation of both projects and roadmaps. Internal audits are regularly conducted to assess project implementation and validate compliance with enterprise standards.

Pernod closely monitors its terroirs, a term it uses to delineate geographic regions based on their soil, biodiversity, climate and other natural factors that imbue agricultural products, like wine, with distinct character. Pernod performs a terroir-mapping exercise that involves registering all terroirs by ingredient and location, isolating priority terroirs and analyzing eight environmental and nine social risks at the terroir level for each priority terroir. Currently, 28 out of 60 priority terroirs are in water basins exposed to water-security risks.

Risk Analysis Determines Priorities

These assessments are combined into a risk score for all priority terroirs, with those in India, Armenia, Mexico and China facing the highest exposure to water risks. Although risk identification is top-down, risks are managed locally.

Agronomic and environmental experts collaborate with local procurement teams, who often work with trusted NGOs to support farmers. An internal data-management platform maps water risks to GPS coordinates, enabling shared understanding of risks across Pernod’s local teams and farmers. Pernod aims to empower, train or support over 5,000 farmers on regenerative agriculture and biodiversity preservation, including water management, by 2030.

Operational units located in high-risk watersheds must classify water challenges, characterize the local context, confer with local partners and initiate appropriate water- balance projects. Pernod India, for example, collaborates with local communities on new water-replenishment projects. In the crop-producing region of Dera Bassi, Punjab, Pernod launched Project Srijal to generate 33.1 million liters via the establishment of a check dam, a farm pond, injection wells and rainwater-harvesting systems, together with desilting dilapidated ponds. Project Srijal also features awareness sessions with small- and medium-scale farmers on water management and wheat- and rice-growing techniques, aiming to increase yield by 20% at minimal cost.25

Following our engagement with Pernod, we believe the company’s terroir mapping enables thorough risk identification and management. The company allocates resources to leverage this mapping by enlisting experts to collaborate with procurement teams and operational units in implementing tailored solutions. While Pernod’s goal to assist 5,000 farmers is company-wide, decisions regarding the scope and nature of stakeholder involvement in water-management plans are primarily made at the local level. Although this tailored approach acknowledges the site-specific nature of risks associated with water and affected communities, we believe there is still potential for broader systemic integration of local stakeholder mapping and engagement.

Analog Devices: Reducing Water Intensity in Chipmaking

Manufacturing semiconductors is a water-intensive business. For a chipmaker like Analog Devices (ADI), water risk management is crucial for ensuring business resilience. The US multinational, based in Wilmington, Massachusetts, recognizes that regions once considered immune to water scarcity are now grappling with water-security challenges.

Since joining the company in 2021, the global head of operations (GHO), mindful of this evolving risk landscape, has made water management a priority. Under his leadership, the company set a new water target that aims to reduce water intensity by 50% by 2027, compared to a 2022 baseline.

This target goes beyond the previous water reuse targets and comes at a pivotal moment for the company. ADI is working to double its manufacturing capacity with expansions in the US Northwest. As the business expands, ADI is reconsidering how and where water is used. It also aims to ensure that new infrastructure fosters smarter consumption by, for example, eliminating idle flows, reducing the frequency of wafer rinses and lengthening rinse tank renewal cycles.

ADI harnesses the expertise of its local workforce to implement its water strategy. Facility-organization and manufacturing- operations teams identify water-saving initiatives, which are reviewed quarterly by senior leadership. In 2023, ADI formed water teams at each fabrication facility in Washington, Oregon, Massachusetts and Limerick, Ireland, to foster collaboration on water conservation projects.

Additionally, a cross-functional water-reduction team was launched to wed local expertise with macro visibility and facilitate shared learning. This team meets monthly and includes representatives from various departments, ranging from manufacturing to engineering to business functions. Ultimately, the GHO is accountable for executing the water strategy.

ADI recognizes the importance of considering its water usage in relation to a municipality’s water needs. The company collaborates with municipalities and holds informal, ad hoc meetings to discuss water consumption. Enhanced engagement with municipalities is also on the agenda, as ADI perceives an opportunity for municipalities to bolster their planning for future water security.

While ADI is working actively to reduce its water consumption and minimize its impact on neighboring water users, after engaging with the company, we believe there is room for improvement in stakeholder management practices. ADI recognizes the importance of water security and has established both a company-wide target and dedicated site-specific and cross-functional teams to promote water conservation. Although ADI acknowledges water as a shared resource, we think formalizing engagement with local stakeholders as part of its water stewardship could enhance its approach.

Toward Better Water Security

Water is a systemic risk that poses an increasing threat to corporations and investors. When investors encounter

water risks, we can use our five-part investor framework to evaluate companies’ stakeholder management practices, initiate a comprehensive dialogue with company management and encourage them to adopt stakeholder management to mitigate risks.

In our view, assessing risks associated with water and local stakeholders is an important component of our fiduciary duty. We continually aim to enhance our knowledge and analytical methods in an effort to screen for these portfolio risks. Our objective is to make well-informed, risk-adjusted investment decisions and optimize value creation for our clients. As new regulations—such as the CSRD—emerge, we will develop additional tools to assess and promote stakeholder management.

We believe that adopting stakeholder management in conjunction with better disclosure is an essential strategy for mitigating escalating water risks. Neglecting this approach overlooks a vital aspect of water risk management. In our view, investors who champion this combination will be the most successful in mitigating and managing water-related risks that might unknowingly seep into portfolio performance over time.

The value of an investment can go down as well as up, and investors may not get back the full amount they invested. Capital is at risk. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
